elements.sent-2411-001: The set of sums of two squares plays an important role in the elementary number theory.
elements.sent-2411-002: In \cite{Nasso22 , Di Nasso investigated several infinite monochromatic patterns in integers considering operations induced from affine maps and asked whether one can find different sets of infinite monochromatic configurations in natural numbers with the structure induced from the set of sums of two squares.}
elements.sent-2411-003: This article investigates Nasso's question and along the way proves several classical Ramsey-type theorems in this new setting.
elements.sent-2411-004: Finding monochromatic patterns for a colouring of certain algebraic structures has been a long-standing thrust in Ramsey's theory.
elements.sent-2411-005: Among all, there are a few groundbreaking results, such as the van der Waerden theorem, which deals with the existence of monochromatic arithmetic progression for any finite partition of natural numbers.
elements.sent-2411-006: In other words, the family of subsets of natural numbers given by arithmetic progression is partition regular.
elements.sent-2411-007: One notices that the van der Waerden theorem is a finitary Ramsey-type result that only uses the additive structure of $\bN$.
elements.sent-2411-008: The infinitary version is widely known as the Hindman's theorem, and it states that for any finite colouring of $\bN$, an injective sequence exists such that all finite sums are monochromatic.
elements.sent-2411-009: There are many ways to pass from the additive structure of $\bN$ to a multiplicative structure, such as via the maps $n\mapsto p^n$ for any prime $p$.
elements.sent-2411-010: This change in structure suggests the multiplicative van der Waerden theorem, which states that for any finite partition of $\bN$, a monochromatic geometric progression of arbitrary length exists.
elements.sent-2411-011: The patterns that mix natural numbers' additive and multiplicative structures are much more difficult to proceed.
elements.sent-2411-012: The first instance in this direction is due to Bergelson \cite{Ber10 and Hindman \cite{Hind11 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: They independently prove that the configuration $\{a,b,c,d\}$ with a+b = c.d is monochromatic.
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreira proved that the pattern $\{a, a+b, a.b\}$ , and consequently, with J.M.. Barrett and M.
elements.sent-2411-016: Lupini, Moreira showed the pattern \{a, a+b, a+b+a.b\} is monochromatic in \cite{BLM21 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In the area of mixed pattern, the monochromaticity of the configuration $\{a, b, a+b, a.b\}$ is still an open problem.. Recently, D.
elements.sent-2411-018: Nasso developed a new idea to construct a new operation on $\mathbb{Z}$ involving both the additive and multiplicative structure in \cite{Nasso22 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: He considers a class of associative and commutative operations on the integers originating from the affine transformations.
elements.sent-2411-020: For specific cases, the multiplication for these operations gives the symmetric polynomials (these are combinations of old additive and multiplicative structures.) Then, using the algebra of Stone-\v{C ech compactification of this new semigroup, Nasso produced a lot of new monochromatic patterns in integers.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, polynomial extension of some Ramsey theorems have been considered in \cite{CG24 along the line of Nasso.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, it is interesting to investigate the Ramsey-type properties of well-structures.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $\bN_0$ be the set of natural numbers along with zero.
elements.sent-2411-024: Consider the set $S(m,n):=\{x_1^m+\cdots + x_n^m: x_1, \cdots, x_m \in \bN_0\}.$ It is well known from the elementary number theory that $S(2,4)$ is $\bN_0$, $S(2,3)$ is the set of the natural those are not of the form of $4^a (8b +7)$ for some $a, b \in \bN_0$.
elements.sent-2411-025: This set is not multiplicative, whereas $S(2,4)$ is multiplicative but not interesting to us as it is the whole set.
elements.sent-2411-026: Therefore, this article focuses on $\Sigma= S(2,2)$, which is nothing but the set of all natural numbers such that the prime divisors of form $4k+3$ (if any) having even exponents.
elements.sent-2411-027: Monochromatic patterns induced from the set of all sum of two squares, $\Sigma,$ were asked by D.
elements.sent-2411-028: Nasso in \cite{Nasso22 and we answered this question in this article.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this article, we investigate a new structure on natural numbers induced from the set of sums of two squares, $\Sigma$ and with this new algebraic structure along the Stone-\v{C ech compactification of it, we prove several classical results such as Hindman theorem, Deuber theorem, Brauer's theorem, Miliken-Taylor theorem, geo-arithmetic progression, and polynomial van der Waerden theorem.}

elements.sent-2411-110: A partition regular family $\cF$ of $\bN$ is a subset of $\cP_f (\bN)$ such that for any partition $\bN= C_1 \cup \cdots \cup C_r$, there exists an $i \in \{1, \cdots, r \}$ such that $C_i \in \cF.$.
elements.sent-2411-111: For any finite coloring of $L(\A)$, there exist $\alpha \in L(\A)$, $\gamma \in \cP_f(\bN)$ and $F \in \cF$ such that $\Dom(\alpha), \gamma, F$ are pairwise disjoint sets and \begin{equation}\{\alpha \cup (\gamma \cup \{t\} \times \{s\}): s \in \A , t \in F\}\end{equation} is monochromatic.

elements.sent-2411-122: Fix $k \in \mathbb{N ,$ let $\mathcal{F = \{\{a, a+d, \cdots, a+kd\} : a,d \in \mathbb{N}\}$ be the set of all $(k+1)$-term arithmetic progressions, put $\cA=\{0,1, \cdots, k\}$ and define \begin{equation}h : L(\cA) \rightarrow N by h(\alpha)= \underset{t \in \Dom(\alpha)}{\ast_f} t^{(\alpha(t))}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: We color each $\alpha \in L(\cA)$ with the color of $h(\alpha)$ and choose $\alpha, \gamma$ and $F= \{a, a+d, \cdots, a+kd\}.$ Then using the Theorem \ref{Beiglbock we obtain that the set \begin{equation}\{h(\alpha \cup (\gamma \cup \{a+id\})\times \{j\}) : i,j \in \{0,1, \cdots, k\}\end{equation} is monochromatic.. \begin{equation}h(\alpha \cup (\gamma \cup \{a+id\})\times \{j\}) = (\underset{t \in \Dom(\alpha)}{\ast_f}t^{(\alpha(t))}) \ast_f (\underset{t \in \gamma}{\ast_f} t^{(j)}) \ast_f ((a+id)^{(j)}) = \card (\Sigma/{({\prod_{t \in \Dom(\alpha)}{s_t^{\alpha(t)}}} )({\prod_{t \in \gamma}{s_t}} {s_{a+id}} )^j}).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-125: Polynomial extension of van der Waerden's theorem relies on the polynomial version of the Hales-Jewett theorem.. In 1988, V.

elements.sent-2411-139: Therefore the image $m (\{ a\oplus x_1 \gamma \oplus \cdots \oplus x_d \gamma^d : (x_1, \cdots x_d) \in [q]^d\})$ is monochromatic for the coloring $\chi$ of $\bN.$ Note that \begin{equation}m( a\oplus x_1 \gamma \oplus \cdots \oplus x_d \gamma^d) = \overset{d}{\underset{j=1}{\af}}(\underset{{(i_1, \cdots i_j) \in [N]^j}}{\af} \Vec{b}_j((i_1, \cdots i_j))) =( \overset{d}{\underset{j=1}{\af}}(\underset{{(i_1, \cdots i_j) \in \gamma^j}}{\af}\Vec{b}_j((i_1, \cdots i_j)) )\ast_f (\overset{d}{\underset{j=1}{\af}}(\underset{{(i_1, \cdots i_j) \in [N]^j \setminus \gamma^j}}{\af}\Vec{b}_j((i_1, \cdots i_j))) = x_1^{(c)}\ast_f \cdots \ast_f x_d^{(c^d)} \ast_f b = \card(S/ s_b s_{x_1}^{c}\cdots s_{x_d}^{c^d}).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-141: Let $(P,<)$ be a countably infinite poset.
elements.sent-2411-142: Then there is a canonical bijection \begin{equation}\phi: P \to \bN given by \phi(x)= \card \{y \in P: y < x\},\end{equation} with an inverse $\psi$ given by $\psi(n)= (n+1)^{st}$ smallest element of $P.$ Since $\psi$ does not preserve the multiplication of $\bN$, hence if $P$ is a countable multiplicative subgroup of $\bN$, then the pullback $\psi$ is not a semigroup homomorphism with respect to the usual multiplication.

elements.sent-2411-145: The set $\Sigma=\{ a^2 +b^2 : a, b \in \bN_0\}$ is symmetric with respect to $a,b$, therefore there is canonical bijection between the set $T:= \{(a,b)\in \bN_0^2: a \le b\}$ and $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-146: Then the map $\psi: T \to \bN$ given by $\psi(m, 2n)= (n+1)^2-m$ and $\psi(m, 2n+1)= (n+1)(n+2)-m$ given a bijection which induces a new operation on $T.$ Hence, one may write a lot of different monochromatic configurations in $\bN.$.
